summary: Hotz introduces 'person of compute' as a unit of measurement (20 PFLOPS) to contextualize AI computing power in human-scale terms. He calculates that LLaMA took about 2 person-years to train while GPT-4 required roughly 100 person-years. Using Moore's Law projections, he estimates when various scales of compute will become accessible—from Google's current 450-person computer to a hypothetical 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion person-equivalents). He concludes with a timeline suggesting desktop-scale 'Humanity' compute by 2089, expressing impatience with the pace.
key_points: A 'person of compute' equals 20 PFLOPS (64 A100s), consuming about 30kW—making AI training measurable in familiar human labor terms
LLaMA required 2 person-years of compute while GPT-4 took approximately 100 person-years, showing current models remain at human-comprehensible scales
Money can buy time: doubling your budget buys you 2 years of Moore's Law; Facebook is effectively 16 years ahead, Google 24 years
At ISS-level spending ($100B), humanity could build a '400,000 person' or 'Tampa' of compute—currently our ceiling
A 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion persons) could sit under your desk by 2089 if Moore's Law holds
money_quotes: We will define one person of compute as 20 PFLOPS (64 A100s, or a single dense 42U A100 rack).
As long as these things are around human scale (for the next 10 years), I think these units make sense.
2038 remains my lifelong estimate for what most people consider the Singularity. I like that it's the Unix timestamp rollover
Today, you can buy two years by doubling your budget.
The most expensive thing humanity has built is the ISS at $100B. If we built a computer at that scale, it would be 400,000 people.
That's one Tampa of compute, which is the most we could hope to build today. A single Tampa.
And in 66 years, you'll have a Humanity under your desk.
key_insight: Framing compute in human-equivalent units reveals that today's largest AI models are still at comprehensible human scales, but Moore's Law will deliver desktop-level 'Humanity' compute within a lifetime.
